Fact: Its flag has been redesigned more than any other country's — over 20 versions since 1900.
Albania was the world's first officially atheist state in 1967; the double-headed eagle on its flag is a medieval symbol dating back to Byzantine times.
Africa's largest country by area; the red crescent on its flag was added to emphasize Islamic identity upon independence from France in 1962.
A co-principality governed jointly by the French president and the Spanish Bishop of Urgell; it has no airport and no standing army.
The machete and gear on its flag reflect Marxist-Leninist influences at independence in 1975; it has the second-largest Portuguese-speaking population in the world after Brazil.
Its rising-sun flag symbolizes the dawn of independence; the islands reportedly have 365 beaches — one for every day of the year.
The 'Sun of May' on its flag commemorates the 1810 revolution against Spain; Argentina is the largest Spanish-speaking country in the world by area.
Armenia was the first country to adopt Christianity as its state religion in 301 AD; the red stripe on its flag represents the Armenian Highland and the struggle for survival.
The Southern Cross on its flag is visible only from the Southern Hemisphere; Australia is the only country that occupies an entire continent.
Legend says its red-white-red flag was inspired by Duke Leopold V's blood-soaked tunic after the Battle of Acre in 1191 — one of history's oldest flag origin stories.
Called the 'Land of Fire' due to naturally occurring flame seeps; its first oil well predates Pennsylvania's famous 1859 well by over a decade.
Comprises over 700 islands with only about 30 inhabited; the blue on its flag represents the sea that defines Bahamian life.
The only island nation in the Middle East; the five white serrations on its flag represent the five pillars of Islam.
Home to the world's largest river delta — the Sundarbans; the red disc on its flag represents the sun rising over the green landscape.
Became a republic in 2021 removing the British monarch as head of state; the trident on its flag was deliberately broken from a colonial 'Britannia' design to symbolize independence.
The Basque language (Euskara) is a language isolate — completely unrelated to any other known language in the world.
One of the last countries in Europe with a KGB secret police; its red-and-green flag was restored from the Soviet era after a 1995 referendum.
Its flag colors come from the medieval Duchy of Brabant's coat of arms; Belgium holds the world record for the longest period without a government — 541 days in 2010–2011.
The only country in Central America with an English-speaking majority; its flag is the only national flag in the world that depicts humans.
The former Kingdom of Dahomey here was one of West Africa's most powerful pre-colonial states and a major hub of the transatlantic slave trade.
One of the few carbon-negative countries in the world; the Druk (Thunder Dragon) on its flag gives the country its local name 'Druk Yul' — Land of the Thunder Dragon.
The only landlocked country in South America with a naval force; it lost its Pacific coast to Chile in 1884 and maintains a navy to this day as a statement of intent.
Its blue-and-gold flag evokes European colors; Sarajevo was the site of the 1914 assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand which triggered World War I.
One of Africa's greatest conservation success stories with 38% of its land protected; the black and white stripes on its flag symbolize racial harmony and the zebra.
In 2019 a referendum saw 98% of Bougainvilleans vote for independence from Papua New Guinea — one of the most decisive independence mandates in history.
The stars on its flag depict the night sky over Rio de Janeiro on November 15 1889 (the date of the republic's proclamation); it is the world's largest Portuguese-speaking country.
The Breton Gwenn-ha-du flag features an ermine pattern from the medieval Duchy of Brittany; Breton is a Celtic language closely related to Welsh and Cornish.
One of the world's wealthiest nations per capita due to oil wealth; the crest on its flag contains an umbrella symbolizing the sultan's supreme authority.
The Cyrillic alphabet was developed by disciples of Saints Cyril and Methodius in Bulgaria in the 9th century; Bulgaria has never changed its name since its founding.
Its name means 'Land of Upright People'; the red star on its flag represents the 1983 revolution and the guiding principles of the nation.
Its flag features three stars representing the three main ethnic groups — Hutu, Tutsi, and Twa; it is one of the world's most densely populated countries.
An archipelago with no indigenous population before Portuguese colonists arrived in 1456; it is known for remarkable political stability in an often turbulent region.
The only country whose flag features a building — Angkor Wat, the world's largest religious monument.
Called 'Africa in miniature' for its stunning geographic and cultural diversity; its name comes from 'Rio dos Camarões' (River of Prawns) named by Portuguese explorers.
The world's second-largest country by area; the maple leaf on its flag was chosen in 1965 after one of history's most heated national flag debates.
Catalonia has its own language, parliament, and police force; Barcelona's economy alone rivals that of many mid-sized European countries.
A vertical red stripe uniquely unites the four horizontal stripes on its flag representing blood, tolerance, forests, and savanna.
Chad's flag is virtually identical to Romania's — both are vertical blue-yellow-red tricolors — differing only in a slight shade of blue.
The world's longest and narrowest country at 4,270 km long but averaging only 177 km wide; its flag is often said to have inspired the Texas state flag.
The large yellow star on its flag represents the Communist Party; China has the world's oldest continuous civilization spanning over 4,000 years.
Named after Christopher Columbus; it is the only South American country with both a Pacific and Caribbean coastline.
Its flag has four stars and stripes for its four islands — three of which form Comoros while the fourth (Mayotte) remains a French territory.
The Republic of Congo and the Democratic Republic of Congo are the only two countries in the world to share the same name and face each other across a river.
St. Piran's Cross — white on black — is said to represent silver tin ore emerging from black rock; Cornwall was once the world's leading center of tin and copper mining.
Abolished its standing army in 1948; it generates nearly 100% of its electricity from renewable sources and dedicates 25% of its land to national parks.
Croats are credited with inventing the necktie; the red-and-white checkerboard coat of arms on its flag is one of Europe's oldest heraldic designs.
The lone star on its flag represents independence; Cuba has more doctors per capita than almost any other country in the world.
The only country whose flag features a map of the country itself — shown in copper-orange with olive branches representing peace.
Prague is one of the few major European capitals that survived World War II largely intact; the Czech Republic produces more beer per capita than any country on Earth.
Africa's second-largest country by area and home to the Congo River Basin — the world's second-largest tropical rainforest.
The Danish Dannebrog is the world's oldest national flag still in continuous use — legend places its first sighting at the Battle of Lyndanisse in 1219.
One of the hottest countries on Earth; it simultaneously hosts military bases from the US, France, China, and Japan — more foreign military presences than almost anywhere.
The only national flag in the world to feature the color purple — in the feathers of the endangered Sisserou parrot; the island has no sandy beaches.
The only national flag to feature a Bible; it shares the island of Hispaniola with Haiti and is home to the Caribbean's highest mountain peak.
One of the world's youngest countries (independent 2002); the black triangle on its flag represents colonialism to overcome while yellow symbolizes prosperity.
Named after the equator which runs through it; Ecuador is one of 17 'megadiverse' countries and home to the Galápagos Islands.
Home to one of the world's oldest civilizations; the Eagle of Saladin on its flag commemorates the famous 12th-century Muslim military leader.
The smallest and most densely populated country in Central America; its flag is the only Central American one to include the phrase 'Dios Unión Libertad' (God Union Liberty).
The only Spanish-speaking country in Africa; the green triangle on its flag represents the country's tropical forests and natural wealth.
The olive wreath on its flag has exactly 30 leaves — one for each year of the independence war against Ethiopia that ended in 1991.
The first country in the world to use internet voting in national elections in 2005; its blue-black-white flag symbolizes sky, soil, and the aspiration for a bright future.
One of the world's last absolute monarchies; the shield and spears on its flag represent the protection and warrior heritage of the Swazi people.
The only African country never colonized by a European power; Ethiopia's green-yellow-red tricolor directly inspired the flags of dozens of other African nations.
One of only a few flags to use light blue as the field color; Fiji comprises over 330 islands of which about 110 are inhabited.
Finland has over 188,000 lakes — more than almost any country on Earth; Santa Claus is officially based in Rovaniemi in Finnish Lapland.
The most visited country in the world; the French tricolor adopted during the Revolution became the template for dozens of national flags worldwide.
Over 80% of Gabon is covered by tropical forest; the blue stripe on its flag represents the Atlantic Ocean which forms its entire western border.
The smallest country on mainland Africa; the Gambia River bisects it entirely giving the country its distinctive narrow elongated shape.
One of the world's oldest wine-producing cultures with 8,000-year-old traditions; its Five Cross Flag is one of the few national flags to feature four additional crosses.
The black-red-gold flag dates to the 1848 democratic revolution; Germany has the world's fourth-largest economy and is home to over 1,500 breweries.
The first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence (1957); the black star on its flag is the Pan-African symbol of liberation used by Marcus Garvey.
The birthplace of democracy, the Olympics, and Western philosophy; the nine blue-and-white stripes on its flag represent the syllables of the Greek for 'Freedom or Death.'
Known as the 'Spice Isle' for its nutmeg production; its flag is the only national flag in the world to depict a spice — a nutmeg in the left stripe.
Home to one of the ancient Maya civilization's greatest cities; its flag is the only one to feature a quetzal bird — the most colorful bird in Central America.
Contains the source of three major West African rivers; its red-yellow-green Pan-African colors are intentionally the reverse of the French tricolor.
One of the world's ten poorest countries; the black star and red stripe on its flag were directly inspired by the flag of Ghana.
The only English-speaking country in South America; the 'Golden Arrowhead' design on its flag symbolizes mineral wealth and a forward-moving people.
The only country formed through a successful slave revolt; the blue and red on its flag come from the French tricolor with the white — representing colonizers — stripped out.
The white bauhinia flower on its flag is Hong Kong's city emblem; it has more skyscrapers than any other city in the world.
Named by Columbus from the Spanish for 'depths' referring to deep coastal waters; the five stars on its flag represent the original five states of Central America.
Hungarian (Magyar) is one of Europe's few non-Indo-European languages; its red-white-green tricolor was adopted during the revolution of 1848 against Habsburg rule.
Home to the world's oldest parliament (the Althing, founded in 930 AD); Iceland has never fought in a war in modern history.
The world's most populous democracy; the blue Ashoka Chakra on its flag has 24 spokes representing the 24 hours of the day — symbolizing constant progress.
The world's largest archipelago nation with over 17,000 islands; its simple red-and-white flag is one of the oldest national flag designs in Southeast Asia.
Formerly known as Persia; the phrase 'Allahu Akbar' is written 22 times on its flag to mark the 22nd of Bahman — the exact date of the 1979 revolution.
Home to ancient Mesopotamia — one of the world's first civilizations; Baghdad was the largest city in the world during the 8th and 9th centuries.
The only country to have a musical instrument — the harp — as its national symbol; its green-white-orange tricolor was inspired by the French Revolution flag.
The triskelion on its flag — three armored legs — is one of the world's most unusual flag symbols; its parliament (Tynwald) is the world's oldest continuous parliament founded in 979 AD.
Home to more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country; Italy's tricolor directly inspired the flags of Mexico, Hungary, and many other nations.
Shares almost identical flag colors with Ireland but in reverse order; it is the world's largest producer of cocoa supplying about 40% of the global crop.
The only national flag in the world that contains neither red, white, nor blue; the gold, black, and green represent the sun, hardships, and the lush land.
The Hinomaru (Circle of the Sun) has been used as a flag since at least the 13th century; Japan is home to the world's oldest known company — Kongo Gumi (construction, founded 578 AD).
The seven-pointed star on its flag represents the seven verses of the Quran's opening chapter and the seven hills of Amman; it hosts more Syrian refugees per capita than any other country.
The world's largest landlocked country; the golden sun and steppe eagle on its blue flag represent freedom and the vast open steppes.
Straddles the equator; the Maasai shield and crossed spears on its flag represent defense of freedom and reflect Kenya's warrior traditions.
The only country in the world with land in all four hemispheres; the rising sun on its flag depicts the equator on which much of the nation sits.
The Kurds are the world's largest ethnic group without their own nation-state; the 21-point sun on the Kurdish flag is an ancient Zoroastrian symbol of light and life.
One of the world's wealthiest countries per capita; it was the first Gulf state to adopt a written constitution and parliament in 1962.
The yellow symbol on its flag is a top-down view of the crown of a traditional yurt; the name 'Kyrgyzstan' roughly translates to 'Land of Forty Tribes.'
The only landlocked country in Southeast Asia; the white disc on its flag represents the full moon reflected in the Mekong River.
Latvia's dark-red-white-dark-red flag is one of the world's oldest — a design from around 1280 AD may make it older than Denmark's famous Dannebrog.
The cedar tree on its flag has been a symbol of Lebanon for 5,000 years and is mentioned in the Bible over 70 times.
The only country in the world where the entire territory lies above 1,000 meters in elevation; the Basotho hat on its flag is a traditional conical woven straw hat.
Founded by freed American slaves in 1847; its flag closely mirrors the US flag with 11 stripes representing the signatories of its Declaration of Independence.
Was the world's only national flag with just a single solid color — all green — from 1977 to 2011 under Muammar Gaddafi.
At the 1936 Olympics Liechtenstein and Haiti discovered their flags were identical; Liechtenstein added a golden crown the very next day.
Called the 'Amber Capital of the World'; its yellow-green-red flag represents wheat, forests, and the blood of those who died for freedom.
Maintains its own unique language (Luxembourgish) despite bordering France, Belgium, and Germany; the Grand Duke receives the largest official salary of any European monarch.
The world's most densely populated territory; Macau generates more casino revenue than Las Vegas and its flag features the lotus flower and the historic Guia Lighthouse.
A biodiversity hotspot where over 90% of its wildlife exists nowhere else on Earth; the red and white on its flag represent the historic Merina kingdom.
Lake Malawi contains more species of freshwater fish than any other lake in the world; a rising sun was added to its flag in 2010 to symbolize economic progress.
Its flag is modeled after the US flag and features 14 stripes and a 14-pointed star representing the original 13 states plus the federal government.
The world's lowest-lying country with an average ground elevation of just 1.5 meters; it is among the countries most threatened by rising sea levels.
Home to the ancient Mali Empire — once the wealthiest empire in the world under Mansa Musa; its flag uses simple Pan-African colors with no emblem.
The George Cross on its flag was awarded by King George VI for extraordinary Maltese bravery during the WWII Siege of Malta in 1942.
The rising diagonal stripe on its flag represents the equator; the islands were the site of 67 US nuclear weapons tests between 1946 and 1958.
One of the last countries to officially abolish slavery — in 1981 and criminalized only in 2007; its crescent and star are set against Pan-African colors.
Has no indigenous population; its four-stripe flag represents the four main ethnic communities; Mauritius is the only African country with a Hindu majority.
The eagle devouring a serpent on its flag comes from an Aztec legend about the founding of Tenochtitlán (modern Mexico City); Mexico introduced chocolate and corn to the world.
The four stars on its flag represent its four main island groups; the federation occupies over 2.6 million square kilometers of ocean despite its tiny land area.
Home to the world's largest wine cellar — the Cricova winery with 120 km of underground tunnels; its flag is the Romanian tricolor with a national coat of arms.
The second-smallest country in the world and the most densely populated; its red-and-white flag is identical in design to Indonesia's but with different proportions.
Home to the Mongol Empire — the largest contiguous land empire in history; the Soyombo symbol on its flag encodes fire, sun, moon, earth, water, and yin-yang.
Its name means 'Black Mountain' in Slavic; it restored an 1852-era coat of arms to its flag when it declared independence from Serbia in 2006.
The only African country with both Atlantic and Mediterranean coastlines; the pentagram on its flag represents the Seal of Solomon.
The only national flag in the world to feature an AK-47 assault rifle — symbolizing defense of freedom — alongside a book for education and a hoe for agriculture.
Has more Buddhist pagodas than any other country on Earth; the white star on its 2010 flag represents the unity of the nation.
Home to the Namib Desert — the world's oldest desert at around 55 million years old; the sun on its flag represents the country's harsh but beautiful landscape.
The world's third-smallest country and smallest island nation; the yellow line and star on its flag show its position just south of the equator.
The only country in the world with a non-rectangular flag — its unique double-pennant shape combines a Hindu sun symbol and a Buddhist moon.
The original Dutch orange-white-blue flag inspired the tricolors of Russia, France, and many other nations; the Netherlands is the world's second-largest food exporter after the US.
Holds roughly 25% of the world's known nickel reserves; three referendums on independence from France were held between 2018 and 2021 with all narrowly rejected.
The first country in the world to grant women the right to vote in 1893; the Southern Cross on its flag represents the Southern Hemisphere.
One of only two flags to feature the color purple (the other is Dominica); a rainbow over a volcano on its coat of arms represents peace and natural riches.
The largest country in West Africa; the orange on its flag represents the Sahara while the white represents purity and the green the fertile south.
Africa's most populous country and largest economy; its plain green-and-white flag has no coat of arms — the design was submitted by a student in a 1959 competition.
One of the world's most isolated countries; its red star on a white disc framed by blue and white stripes is a design created under Soviet influence in 1948.
Its 'Sun of Liberty' flag was adopted after a dispute with Greece over the name 'Macedonia'; Alexander the Great was born in what is now this region.
Canada's newest and largest territory created in 1999; its flag features an Inuksuk stone landmark and the North Star — symbols of Inuit culture and guidance.
Has the world's largest sovereign wealth fund; the Norwegian Nordic cross subtly contains elements of the flags of Denmark, Sweden, and Finland within its design.
One of the oldest continuously inhabited regions on Earth; the khanjar (curved dagger) on its flag is a symbol of Omani national identity and honor.
Ethiopia's largest region and home to the Oromo people — the country's largest ethnic group; the Odaa tree on its flag is a sacred symbol of the Oromo Gadaa democratic assembly system.
The white stripe on its flag represents religious minorities; Pakistan is home to K2 — the world's second-highest mountain and considered by many climbers more dangerous than Everest.
Its full-moon-on-blue flag features the disc slightly off-center for aesthetic balance; Palau's Rock Islands are a UNESCO World Heritage Site and world-class diving destination.
The black-white-green with red triangle reflects Pan-Arab nationalist colors used since the 1916 Arab Revolt; Palestine has been recognized as a state by over 140 countries.
Its four-quadrant flag features a blue star for the Conservative Party and a red star for the Liberal Party; the Panama Canal handles about 5% of all global maritime trade.
Home to the world's most linguistically diverse nation — over 800 languages spoken; its flag features the Bird of Paradise and the Southern Cross.
The only country in the world with a different design on each side of its flag — the national seal on the front and the treasury seal on the back.
The red and white on its flag reportedly come from flamingos seen flying overhead by liberator José de San Martín; Machu Picchu is one of the world's greatest archaeological wonders.
The only Asian flag flipped upside down during wartime — moving the red stripe to the top; it has more islands (7,641) than any other country in Asia.
Poland's white-and-red flag dates to a 13th-century coat of arms; Polish mathematicians cracked the Enigma code before WWII — a feat overlooked for decades.
Has the world's oldest national borders — unchanged since 1139 AD; the armillary sphere on its flag represents Portugal's Age of Discovery and global maritime empire.
Puerto Ricans are US citizens who cannot vote in presidential elections; its flag was deliberately modeled on the Cuban flag with the triangle colors inverted.
The unique maroon color on its flag is found on no other national flag; Qatar is the world's largest exporter of liquefied natural gas (LNG) per capita.
The only Canadian province with a French-speaking majority; it held two independence referendums in 1980 and 1995 — the 1995 vote failed by less than 1%.
Romania's flag is nearly identical to Chad's — both are vertical blue-yellow-red tricolors — leading to occasional diplomatic confusion at international events.
The largest country in the world spanning 11 time zones; its white-blue-red tricolor directly inspired the flags of many other Slavic nations.
Rebuilt from the devastating 1994 genocide to become one of Africa's fastest-growing economies; the sun on its flag symbolizes enlightenment and a new era of transparency.
The smallest country in the Western Hemisphere; the two white stars on its flag represent hope and liberty for each of its two islands.
The black and white triangle on its flag symbolizes cultural harmony; the yellow background represents sunshine and the famous twin Pitons — volcanic peaks recognized as a UNESCO site.
The three green diamonds in a 'V' shape on its flag represent the 'Gems of the Antilles'; the country grows over 90% of the world's arrowroot.
One of the first countries to greet each new year; the Southern Cross on its flag has guided Pacific Islander navigation for thousands of years.
Claims to be the world's oldest republic — founded in 301 AD; it is completely surrounded by Italy and has no access to the sea.
Has the highest biodiversity density in Africa; the three stars on its flag represent its main islands including the uninhabited Rolas Island.
The Shahada (Islamic declaration of faith) on its flag makes it one of the few to include religious text; it contains the world's largest oil field — the Ghawar Field.
The Saltire (St. Andrew's Cross) is one of the oldest national flags in the world dating to 832 AD; Scotland is credited with inventing golf, the telephone, television, and penicillin.
The green star on its flag represents Islam and progress; Dakar sits at the westernmost point of mainland Africa and was a key hub for trans-Atlantic trade.
Nikola Tesla was born in what is now Serbia; Serbia's coat of arms with the double-headed eagle is one of Europe's oldest continuous heraldic traditions.
Its flag's radiating stripes from a corner are unique among all national flags; the Seychelles is Africa's smallest country by both area and population.
Its flag features the Trinacria — a three-legged figure with Medusa's head surrounded by wheat — one of the world's most distinctive and ancient regional flag symbols.
Its capital Freetown was founded in 1787 as a settlement for freed slaves; the green-white-blue flag represents forests, unity, and the sea.
One of only three city-states in the world; the crescent and five stars on its flag represent a young nation and its five ideals: democracy, peace, progress, justice, and equality.
The double cross on its coat of arms represents the Byzantine missionaries Cyril and Methodius who brought Christianity and literacy to the region in the 9th century.
The Triglav mountain on its coat of arms is its highest peak; Slovenia was the first Yugoslav republic to join both the EU and NATO in 2004.
The five stars on its flag represent the five main island groups; Guadalcanal here was the site of a turning-point campaign in the Pacific theater of World War II.
The blue on its flag was inspired by the UN flag in gratitude for the UN's role in Somali independence; the five-pointed star represents the five historical Somali territories.
Declared independence from Somalia in 1991 and operates as a stable de facto state with its own elections, currency, and military — yet remains unrecognized by any UN member state.
The only country to have voluntarily built then dismantled its nuclear weapons; its flag features more colors than any other national flag.
The Taegukgi flag's yin-yang represents cosmic balance; South Korea transformed from one of the world's poorest economies to a trillion-dollar powerhouse in a single generation.
The world's newest country having gained independence from Sudan in 2011; the gold star on its flag symbolizes the unity of its states.
The Pillars of Hercules on its coat of arms represent the entrance to the Mediterranean at Gibraltar; Spain has the second-most UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Europe.
The Lion Flag is over 2,500 years old — one of the world's oldest national symbols; Sri Lanka was one of the first countries in the world to elect a female head of government in 1960.
Sudan has more ancient pyramids than Egypt — over 200 Nubian pyramids scattered across the desert; the Nile River flows through its heart.
The only sovereign state outside Africa where the majority of the population is of Indian and Indonesian descent; the green star on its flag represents unity and hope.
Home to global brands Ikea, H&M, Spotify, and Minecraft; the Nordic Cross on its blue-and-gold flag is one of Scandinavia's oldest cross design traditions.
Has been officially neutral in international conflicts since 1815; it is one of only two square national flags in the world — the other being Vatican City.
Home to Damascus — one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities; the two green stars on its flag represent Syria and Egypt from the era of the United Arab Republic.
Officially the Republic of China; the blue canton with 12-point white sun on its flag was once the symbol of all China — the 12 points represent the 12 hours of the day.
Home to the Pamir Mountains known as the 'Roof of the World'; the seven stars on its flag represent the seven days of the week and the seven regions of the country.
Home to Kilimanjaro (Africa's highest peak) and the Serengeti (site of the world's largest mammal migration); its name combines Tanganyika and Zanzibar.
The only Southeast Asian country never colonized by a European power; the red-white-blue tricolor was adopted in 1917 partly to show solidarity with WWI allies.
The Tibetan Snow Lion flag — featuring two lions and a rising sun — is banned in China; Tibet's plateau averages over 4,500 meters in elevation making it the world's highest region.
The five stars on its flag represent the five administrative regions; Togo was one of the first African countries to hold multi-party elections in the early 1990s.
The only Pacific nation that was never formally colonized; the red cross on its flag reflects the centrality of Christianity in Tongan culture and identity.
The birthplace of both calypso music and the steel drum; the black diagonal stripe on its flag represents the islands and the unity of the people.
Was the center of the ancient Carthaginian Empire and Rome's greatest rival; the crescent and star on its flag have been regional symbols for over 800 years.
The only country that bridges two continents (Europe and Asia); the crescent and star on its flag are ancient symbols used in Anatolia long before the Ottoman era.
One of the world's most isolated states; the ornate carpet pattern on its flag is unique among all national flags and represents a 2,800-year-old weaving tradition.
The nine stars on its flag represent its nine inhabited atolls; Tuvalu may become one of the first countries entirely uninhabitable due to rising sea levels.
Home to half of the world's remaining mountain gorilla population; the grey crowned crane on its flag is a symbol of peace found only in eastern and southern Africa.
The largest country entirely within Europe; the blue-and-yellow flag represents the sky over fields of wheat — Ukraine was historically known as the 'breadbasket of Europe.'
Built from desert into a global hub in just 50 years; its flag's four colors (black, white, green, red) are the traditional Pan-Arab colors.
The Union Jack combines three crosses — St. George (England), St. Andrew (Scotland), and St. Patrick (Ireland); Britain industrialized first and once governed one-quarter of the world's land area.
The 50 stars on its flag represent each state; the US flag has been modified 27 times — most recently in 1960 when Hawaii became the 50th state.
The first country to legalize cannabis nationwide; the 'Sun of May' on its flag mirrors Argentina's revolutionary symbol honoring shared independence movements.
Home to Samarkand — one of the world's oldest cities and the jewel of the ancient Silk Road; the 12 stars on its flag represent the months of the year.
The only country in the world to feature a boar's tusk on its national flag — a symbol of wealth and manhood in Melanesian culture.
The world's smallest sovereign state by both area and population; it is one of only two countries with a perfectly square flag — the other being Switzerland.
Birthplace of Simón Bolívar who liberated six South American nations; the eight stars on its flag represent the original provinces that signed its Declaration of Independence.
The gold star on its flag represents the Communist Party and the country's five main social classes; Vietnam contains Hang Sơn Đoòng — the world's largest cave.
The red dragon (Y Ddraig Goch) on its flag is one of the oldest national symbols in Europe; Wales has more castles per square mile than any other country in the world.
Africa's last major unresolved territorial dispute; the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic claims sovereignty while Morocco administers most of the territory.
Formerly two separate countries (North and South Yemen) until unification in 1990; it is home to Socotra Island — a UNESCO site sometimes called the 'Galápagos of the Indian Ocean.'
The flying eagle on its flag represents the ability of the Zambian people to rise above adversity; it has some of Africa's richest copper deposits.
A semi-autonomous region of Tanzania; it was historically the world's largest clove producer and briefly an independent sultanate before merging with Tanganyika in 1964.
Named after Great Zimbabwe — a medieval stone city built without mortar; the Zimbabwe Bird on its flag depicts soapstone carvings found at those ancient ruins.
